title: Variational Approach for Uniform Quantum Permutation Generators
authors: Farzam Nosrati, Nicolás Borrajo, Antonio Fernández Anta, Vincenzo Mancuso
abstract: Uniform permutation generation is a fundamental task in both classical and quantum computation, with applications ranging from cryptography to quantum optimization and quantum error correction. Existing exact quantum constructions typically require all-to-all qubit connectivity and quadratic circuit depth. We develop a variational quantum circuit framework for uniform permutation generation under connectivity constraints, in which the circuit architecture is determined by the underlying interaction graph and the variational parameters are optimized to enforce the target permutation statistics. In particular, we present explicit controlled-SWAP-based unitary constructions that achieve exact uniformity with quadratic circuit size and linear depth \(O(n)\) on linear nearest-neighbor topologies. Our approach, therefore, removes the need for all-to-all connectivity while improving the depth of previous exact constructions by a factor. We further prove that a quantum Beneš-like architecture is intrinsically non-uniform. Despite its logarithmic depth and ability to realize any permutation it cannot generate a uniform distribution over permutations for any choice of variational parameters. These results clarify the role of circuit topology in exact permutation generation and identify variational quantum circuits as a natural framework for hardware-constrained uniform sampling. More broadly, this work suggests that exact uniform permutation generation is a strictly stronger requirement than mere permutation realizability, and lays the groundwork for a formal complexity separation between the two.
summary: This paper introduces a variational quantum circuit approach to generate uniform random permutations on $n$ qubits under realistic nearest-neighbor hardware constraints. It successfully constructs an efficient linear-depth circuit achieving exact uniformity, while rigorously proving that other known universal circuits fail this strict requirement.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of implementing uniform random permutation generation over $S_n$ using quantum circuits while respecting physical hardware connectivity constraints.
main_result: A variational circuit framework was developed that achieves exact uniformity with linear depth, $O(n)$, on linear nearest-neighbor topologies, significantly improving upon previous all-to-all constructions. Furthermore, it proves that certain architectures (like the quantum Beneš circuit) can realize permutations but fail to generate a uniform distribution.
method: The authors utilize Variational Quantum Circuits (VQC) parameterized by angles ($\Theta$) and optimize them using projection loss functions ($L_{proj}$) to enforce the target state being proportional to the symmetric projector $\Pi_{sym}$.
model_or_system: The system is a set of $n$ qubits representing subsystems whose permutations are generated by controlled-SWAP gates. The analysis focuses on circuit architectures constrained to linear nearest-neighbor interactions.
key_observables: Circuit size (controlled-SWAP count), circuit depth, and the minimization of the projection loss function ($L_{proj}$) towards zero.
important_parameters: The system size $n$, the variational parameters $\Theta$, and the connectivity graph structure (e.g., linear chain).
main_assumptions: It is conjectured that exact uniform generation requires circuit size $\Omega(n^2)$ and depth $\Omega(n)$ for local architectures, contrasting with the logarithmic depth of universal but non-uniform circuits.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the three-stage VQC construction (PCS gates in layers), the recursive structure of the linear quantum circuit ($M_n$), and convergence plots showing loss function minimization for different topologies.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the problem using a general variational framework, then details the specific construction for linear nearest-neighbor topology achieving $O(n)$ depth. It contrasts this with the Beneš network, proving its inherent non-uniformity, and concludes by establishing complexity separations between mere realizability and true uniform sampling.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, hardware-aware roadmap for implementing fundamental quantum tasks like random sampling, which is crucial for building fault-tolerant quantum algorithms and simulating complex many-body dynamics where connectivity matters.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Three-stage variational quantum-circuit construction for uniform permutation generation. (i) Each candidate swap ˆUi j is realized by a parameterized controlled-SWAP (PCS) gate, with the control qubit prepared by ˆRy(θ). (ii) The PCS-layer architecture is determined by an interaction graph G = (V, E); at each layer ℓ, an ordered subset Mℓ⊆E is chosen to specify the active PCS operations. Gray edges represent admissible interactions, and red edges the selected ones. (iii) For the fixed layered architecture, the variational parameters are optimized by minimizing L(Θ), so that the resulting channel approximates the target uniform permutation map.

FIG. 2. Recursive construction of the non-swap probability matrices Mn and their corresponding linear-topology circuits. (Top) Examples for n = 2, 3, 4, 5 are shown. Each Mn is obtained from the upper and lower blocks inherited from Mn−1, shown in blue and gray, together with two newly added anti-diagonals, shown in red. For even n, the numbered anti-diagonal starts with even entries 2, . . . , n followed by odd entries n −1, . . . , 3, while the other anti-diagonal contains only 1s. For odd n, the roles of the two anti-diagonals are interchanged. (Bottom) The associated quantum circuits implementing the same recursive linear topology.
FIG. 3. Projection-loss optimization for the line-topology PCS ansatz on S 8. The best run over multiple random initializations reaches a residual of order 10−16.
FIG. 4. Quantum Beneš circuit for n = 8.

FIG. 5. Optimization of the projection loss Lproj for the selected PCS swap sequence. The curve shows the best run over multiple random initializations. The finite residual indicates that the ansatz cannot exactly realize the symmetric projector, and therefore does not implement the uniform permutation average over S 8.
